pages.full_text:
11 + 12  Abbey of St. Martin of Canigou

[postcard: Saint-Martin du Canigou / Pays Catalan]

olive trees for shade near the top. Canigou is almost on the same longitude as Paris, so was marked on maps in Louis XIV's time. 2700m.
   The Abbey is gorgeous, restored in [20?] + used as a retreat house with Benedictines.
headings: Abbey of St. Martin of Canigou
pages.notes: The top-left annotation '11 + 12' may refer to handwritten diary page numbers for this section heading rather than this physical page. The postcard is a commercial printed postcard glued in, with printed title 'Saint-Martin du Canigou' in decorative script and subtitle 'Pays Catalan'. The year in 'restored in [20?]' is obscured/cut off by the postcard — partially visible as a circled annotation; could be a year-reference or page number.

pages.full_text:
When the last monks left in 1783 (the last 5 asked to leave) the abbey was looted by local people + capitals used to decorate private houses. The [20 circled] bishop persuaded people to give them back. It has been beautifully restored. I liked it alot. Lovely church interior, good lighting & peaceful, eve with [?Tourists]

[postcard: ABBAYE SAINT-MARTIN-DU-CANIGOU — carving of two stone figures]

Carving of incense bearer in funeral of an abbot at St. Martin's at Canigou
photo_captions: Carving of incense bearer in funeral of an abbot at St. Martin's at Canigou
pages.notes: The circled '20' in the text appears to be an annotation or marginal note, possibly a cross-reference or page number rather than part of the narrative sentence. Last word before the postcard reads 'eve with [?Tourists]' — the word after 'with' is partially cut off at the right margin; 'Tourists' is the best reading. The postcard shows a Romanesque stone carving of two figures, printed caption 'ABBAYE SAINT-MARTIN-DU-CANIGOU'.
